title: An Lushan Rebellion begins
summary: On 16 December 755 the Tang general An Lushan, commanding the empire's largest frontier army from his base at Fanyang near modern Beijing, rose in revolt against Emperor Xuanzong. The war that followed lasted seven years, drove the emperor into exile, cost the life of his beloved concubine Yang Guifei, and by some contemporary reckonings killed or displaced tens of millions of people. Tang China survived, but its cosmopolitan golden age did not, and the imperial state never again enjoyed the confident centralism of its first century and a half.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
By 750 the Tang empire, ruled from the twin capitals of Chang'an and Luoyang, was the most populous and probably the wealthiest state in the world. Its census of 754 counted around 52.9 million registered persons, its Silk Road cities were thick with Sogdian, Persian, Uyghur and Arab merchants, and its court had absorbed styles of music, painting, dress and religion from as far away as Sasanian Iran and Gupta India. The emperor Xuanzong, on the throne since 712, had presided over four decades of stability so glittering that his early years were later remembered as the Kaiyuan era of good government.

But the very success of the dynasty had shifted its centre of gravity. Following the reforms of the chancellor Li Linfu, who ruled the bureaucracy from 736 until his death in 752, permanent military governors known as jiedushi were installed on the frontiers. Each commanded professional troops, controlled local taxation and increasingly appointed his own subordinates. By the mid-750s more than half a million soldiers, well over ninety percent of the empire's professional military, lay under nine such jiedushi, most of them stationed against the Turks, Khitans, Tibetans and Uyghurs of the northern and western frontiers. The armies of the interior had rusted away, and the metropolitan garrisons were parade units.

Into this world came An Lushan, a stocky, illiterate general of Sogdian and Turkic Ashina descent born around 703 near Lake Balkash. He had risen through the northern frontier armies on a combination of battlefield ferocity, personal charm and shameless flattery. In 744 he was made jiedushi of Pinglu, in 751 of Fanyang, and in 752 of Hedong as well, giving him command of roughly 164,000 soldiers along the entire north-eastern frontier. Xuanzong, now in his late sixties and increasingly absorbed in his passionate love for the concubine Yang Guifei, adopted the corpulent general as an honorary son; Yang Guifei herself was said to have washed him in a giant baby's tub as a court prank. An Lushan was permitted the extraordinary privilege of riding into the audience hall.

The fragility of the arrangement was clear only in retrospect. Yang Guifei's cousin Yang Guozhong had succeeded Li Linfu as chancellor and was locked in bitter rivalry with An Lushan, whom he denounced repeatedly at court as a traitor in the making. An Lushan, warned by informers, spent 753 and 754 building up his personal retinue of eight thousand Central Asian cavalrymen and stockpiling supplies at Fanyang. When Yang Guozhong sent a summons that An Lushan believed heralded his arrest, he decided to strike first. His pretext was that he was marching on the capital to remove the corrupt chancellor from the emperor's side.

sections.heading: What happened
sections.body:
On 16 December 755, at Fanyang in what is now the Beijing region, An Lushan issued a manifesto proclaiming that a secret imperial edict authorised him to depose Yang Guozhong. His army, perhaps 150,000 strong including large numbers of Khitan, Xi and Sogdian cavalry, marched south through the Hebei plain. City after city surrendered or was stormed. Loyalist commanders such as Yan Gaoqing, the prefect of Changshan, mounted heroic but doomed resistance and were captured and executed. On 18 January 756 An Lushan entered Luoyang, the eastern capital, without significant fight, and on 5 February proclaimed himself emperor of a new Great Yan dynasty.

Chang'an, three hundred kilometres to the west, was defended by the Tongguan pass, a narrow gorge held for months by the veteran general Geshu Han with the last mobile troops the government could scrape together. When Yang Guozhong, doubting Geshu Han's loyalty, forced him against his advice to march out and give battle on 7 July 756, the loyalist army was destroyed and the road to the capital lay open. Xuanzong fled westward on the night of 14 July with a small escort, heading for the sanctuary of Sichuan. At the Mawei relay station a day's ride from the capital, the imperial troops mutinied. They killed Yang Guozhong and demanded the death of Yang Guifei, whom they blamed for the catastrophe; the aged emperor, weeping, gave the order and she was strangled by a court eunuch beneath a Buddhist shrine. The scene became one of the most famous tragedies in Chinese poetry, above all in Bai Juyi's Song of Everlasting Sorrow of 806.

At Lingwu in the Ordos loop the crown prince Li Heng broke away and had himself proclaimed emperor as Suzong, effectively deposing his father. Chang'an fell to An Lushan's general Sun Xiaozhe within days. But the rebels never truly consolidated. An Lushan, blinded by an ulcerous skin disease and increasingly paranoid, was murdered in his bed on the night of 29 January 757 by his own son An Qingxu. An Qingxu was in turn killed in 759 by An Lushan's old lieutenant Shi Siming, who was murdered by his son Shi Chaoyi in 761, who committed suicide when finally cornered by Tang loyalists on 17 February 763. Meanwhile the Tang recovered its two capitals in the winter of 757, largely thanks to the intervention of the Uyghur Khagan Bayanchur, whose cavalry sacked Luoyang so thoroughly that its recovery took generations.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
The demographic figures that survive from the immediate post-war years are among the most contested in world history. The last pre-war census, in 754, recorded roughly 52.9 million registered inhabitants; the first post-war count, in 764, recorded only about 16.9 million, a fall of some 36 million. Some earlier scholars took the figures literally and ranked the An Lushan rebellion among the deadliest events in human history. Modern historians such as Charles Peterson and Denis Twitchett generally regard the number as reflecting the collapse of the fiscal registration system rather than actual deaths on that scale; huge populations had simply escaped from taxation, migrated south, or been swept into the households of magnates whose registers the state could no longer see. Genuine mortality from war, famine and epidemic was nonetheless enormous, probably in the many millions.

At the political level the Tang empire that emerged from the war in 763 was a very different body. The great jiedushi commands had multiplied, and many of the men now holding them were former rebel generals reconciled to Suzong on terms that left them almost sovereign in their provinces. The North China plain, once the empire's tax base, was effectively lost to central control for a century, its taxes retained by regional strongmen who called themselves Tang subjects only when convenient. To pay its remaining armies the government turned to a state salt monopoly perfected by Liu Yan, and to a new twice-yearly tax on land and households introduced by Yang Yan in 780, which shifted the fiscal centre of gravity from persons to property.

The cosmopolitan Silk Road culture of early Tang was one of the war's casualties. The Uyghur cavalry that had helped defeat the rebels stayed on as unpaid guests in Chang'an; suspicion of Central Asian foreigners hardened; and the emperor Wuzong's suppression of Buddhism in 845, though nearly a century later, drew on resentments that traced back to the rebellion. In the south, protected from the fighting and increasingly the economic heart of the empire, the Yangtze basin flourished, and cities such as Yangzhou and Guangzhou grew into major mercantile ports. Chang'an, still a functioning capital, was never again the world city it had been in Xuanzong's early years.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
For later Chinese historians the An Lushan rebellion became the archetypal warning of what happens when an emperor loses himself in love, when a court favours flatterers over honest officials, and when frontier commanders are trusted with too much unmonitored power. The Confucian scholar Sima Guang, writing his great Zizhi Tongjian in the eleventh century, devoted several dense chapters to the rebellion and drew from it lessons on statecraft that Song emperors read carefully. When Zhao Kuangyin founded the Song dynasty in 960 his celebrated "Cup of Wine" strategy, retiring his generals on generous pensions to prevent them from becoming jiedushi in miniature, was explicitly modelled on avoiding another An Lushan.

Literary and artistic memory of the war was even more enduring. Du Fu, one of the greatest of Chinese poets, lived through the rebellion as a minor official and wrote from experience of famine, refugee columns and family separations; his poem Spring View, composed in besieged Chang'an, is still memorised by schoolchildren. Bai Juyi's Song of Everlasting Sorrow made the doomed romance of Xuanzong and Yang Guifei one of the great themes of East Asian literature, imitated in Japanese as the Genji Monogatari and painted repeatedly through the following centuries.

Modern scholarship has recovered some of the perspectives the traditional record obscured. Recent work on Sogdian communities in Tang China has restored the ethnic and religious complexity of An Lushan's world, and comparative historians have set the rebellion against contemporary crises such as the Abbasid revolution in the Islamic west and the byzantine iconoclast wars. What no reinterpretation can undo is the scale of the transformation the war unleashed. The Tang empire lasted until 907, but the golden age that Xuanzong had presided over ended definitively on 16 December 755, when a stout Sogdian general in a windswept garrison near the Great Wall gave the order to march on the capital.

worldContext.text: The Abbasid caliph al-Mansur, only five years into the new dynasty's rule, was consolidating his power at Kufa and about to begin the construction of the round city of Baghdad in 762. In 755 he had his old ally Abu Muslim al-Khurasani assassinated, provoking revolts across Iran. On the western marches, the fugitive Umayyad prince Abd al-Rahman I landed on the Andalusian coast in 755 and by 756 had made himself amir of Cordoba, opening a permanent breach in the caliphate's unity.

In Francia the young Pippin the Short, crowned king in 751, was leading two campaigns into Lombard Italy in 754 and 756 at the pope's request, formally granting the papacy the lands that became the Papal States. His son Charlemagne was still a boy. In Anglo-Saxon Britain King Aethelbald of Mercia was murdered by his own bodyguards at Seckington in 757 and was succeeded after a brief civil war by Offa, who would rule for the rest of the century. Byzantine emperor Constantine V continued his iconoclast policies.

Northern India was fragmented among the Pratihara, Pala and Rashtrakuta kingdoms, whose triangular struggle for control of Kannauj would define the next two centuries. In Kashmir the powerful king Lalitaditya Muktapida had recently died, ending a period of expansive Karkota rule. The Pala king Gopala had just founded his dynasty in Bengal, and Buddhist Nalanda still functioned as an international university drawing students from as far as Sumatra and Tibet.

In North Africa the Berber Ibadi Kharijite states, notably the Rustamid imamate at Tahert founded in 776, would soon carve independent territories out of the Abbasid Maghreb. Further south the trans-Saharan gold trade was strengthening the emerging Wagadu kingdom of Ghana. In Ethiopia Aksum had entered its long decline as the Red Sea trade shifted into Muslim hands, and in the Nile valley the Christian Nubian kingdoms of Makuria and Alodia continued their long coexistence with Islamic Egypt.

In the Maya lowlands the Late Classic was at its brilliant zenith, but the drought and warfare that would depopulate the southern cities were less than a century away. In central Mexico the successor states to Teotihuacan, above all Xochicalco and Cholula, dominated a fragmented Mesoamerican political landscape. On the Peruvian coast the Moche were gone; the highland Wari empire was near its peak, with administrative centres reaching from Cajamarca in the north to Cuzco in the south.

figures.name: An Lushan
figures.role: Tang general of Sogdian and Turkic Ashina descent; jiedushi of Fanyang, Pinglu and Hedong; proclaimed emperor of Yan in early 756; murdered by his own son in January 757

figures.name: Emperor Xuanzong of Tang
figures.role: Long-reigning Tang emperor from 712 to 756; fled Chang'an in July 756 and abdicated in favour of his son at Chengdu

figures.name: Yang Guifei
figures.role: Beloved consort of Emperor Xuanzong; executed by the imperial guard at the Mawei relay station on 15 July 756

figures.name: Yang Guozhong
figures.role: Chancellor and cousin of Yang Guifei; rival of An Lushan whose rivalry precipitated the revolt; killed at Mawei

figures.name: Emperor Suzong
figures.role: Crown prince Li Heng, proclaimed emperor at Lingwu in 756; presided over the recovery of Chang'an and Luoyang with Uyghur help

figures.name: Shi Siming
figures.role: An Lushan's lieutenant who continued the rebellion after 759 and briefly reunified the rebel state; killed by his son in 761

images.caption: Emperor Xuanzong's Flight to Shu, an unsigned Southern Song hanging scroll in ink, colour and gold on silk, showing the imperial party retreating through the mountains after the fall of Chang'an in 756. Metropolitan Museum of Art, New York.
images.credit: Unknown Chinese artist, mid-12th century, via Wikimedia Commons

images.caption: Map of the main campaigns of the An Lushan rebellion, 755 to 763, showing the initial rebel breakthrough from Fanyang and the arc of the Tang recovery.
